plant.description:
Botanical Family: The provided sources do not specify the botanical plant family for walnuts. (Note: Based on general botanical knowledge outside the provided sources, walnuts belong to the walnut family, Juglandaceae, and you may want to independently verify this information).
2. Brief Description and Common Forms
Description: Walnuts are a type of tree nut that is recognized as a biotin-rich whole food for humans3. However, they are entirely inappropriate and potentially dangerous for cats. Walnuts contain very high amounts of oils and fats1. Because cats have delicate digestive systems that are not adapted to processing such high levels of plant-based fats, eating walnuts can make them unwell and specifically trigger pancreatitis (a painful and potentially life-threatening inflammation of the pancreas)14.
Common Forms: In a household, pets most commonly encounter walnuts as whole nuts, mixed into dried fruit and nut snacks (like trail mixes), or baked into human desserts2.

toxins.name: Based on the provided sources, walnuts do not contain a specific chemical "poison" or toxin
Oils and Fats (Lipids)
toxins.description:
in cats: Cats are obligate carnivores with digestive systems that are not well-adapted to processing high amounts of plant-based oils and fats. Ingesting the high fat content found in walnuts causes gastrointestinal upset and can specifically trigger pancreatitis, which is a severe, painful, and potentially life-threatening inflammation of the pancreas12.
Any notes on concentration or potency: The provided sources do not specify an exact toxic dose or threshold for walnuts. They simply note that walnuts contain "high amounts" of oils and fats, which are sufficient to make a cat unwell if ingested
toxins.concentration_notes: or potency: The provided sources do not specify an exact toxic dose or threshold for walnuts. They simply note that walnuts contain "high amounts" of oils and fats, which are sufficient to make a cat unwell if ingested

symptoms.name: If a cat ingests walnuts, the primary risks arise from the physical properties of the nut and its high fat and fiber content
Gastrointestinal Upset
symptoms.onset: Within a few hours of ingestion.
symptoms.notes: The high fiber content and heavy amount of oils in nuts make them very difficult for cats to digest, easily leading to acute digestive distress

symptoms.name: Pancreatitis
symptoms.onset: 24 to 72 hours (or progressively over a few days).
symptoms.notes: Walnuts contain very high amounts of oils and fats, which can trigger a painful inflammation of the pancreas known as pancreatitis1.... The clinical signs of pancreatitis in cats are often vague, subtle, and non-specific; the most common signs are profound lethargy, partial or complete anorexia (loss of appetite), and dehydration, though vomiting and abdominal pain can also occur

symptoms.name: Choking or Intestinal Obstruction
symptoms.onset: Immediate (for choking) or progressive over hours to days (for intestinal blockage).
symptoms.notes: Because nuts are hard and difficult for a cat to digest, intact walnuts or large pieces pose a significant physical hazard. They can become lodged in the throat as a choking hazard or cause dangerous physical blockages within the intestinal tract

treatments.name: Because walnuts do not contain a specific chemical poison and are instead dangerous due to their physical properties and high fat content, there is no specific chemical antidote. Instead
Relief of Choking or Intestinal Obstruction
treatments.description:
of the procedure: If an intact walnut or a large, hard piece becomes lodged in the cat's throat or digestive tract, the veterinarian will need to physically remove the blockage. Depending on the location, this may involve emergency airway clearance, endoscopy, or abdominal surgery.
Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: This is the most urgent intervention. Because walnuts are hard and difficult for cats to digest, they pose an immediate choking hazard and a high risk for intestinal blockages

treatments.name: Intensive Medical Management for Pancreatitis
treatments.description:
of the procedure: If the cat develops acute pancreatitis, they will likely require hospitalization. The veterinarian will administer intravenous (IV) fluids to combat dehydration and correct electrolyte imbalances. They will also prescribe anti-nausea medications (such as maropitant) to stop vomiting and specific pain medications (such as the opioid buprenorphine) to relieve abdominal pain2....
Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: Walnuts contain very high amounts of fat, which can trigger a painful and potentially life-threatening inflammation of the pancreas (pancreatitis)16. Because cats with pancreatitis often refuse to eat, the vet may also use appetite stimulants or place a feeding tube to ensure the cat receives early nutritional support, which is critical for recovery and preventing secondary liver issues

treatments.name: Symptomatic Gastrointestinal Support
treatments.description:
of the procedure: For mild to moderate cases where the walnut has passed safely but caused stomach irritation, the vet may administer subcutaneous fluids (under the skin) for hydration, prescribe anti-nausea medications, and recommend feeding a highly digestible or bland diet until the inflammation subsides4....
Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: The high fiber content and dense oils in nuts make them very difficult for cats to digest, commonly leading to standard stomach upset, indigestion, and diarrhea even if pancreatitis does not develop
